meta.title: G82/G83 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW M4 G82/G83 engine — S58 base, Competition, CSL. Real UK costs, reliability data and common S58 failures. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW M specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M4 G82/G83 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S58 engine for an M4 typically costs £6,000–£12,000 supply only, depending on variant — base and Competition units sit at £6,000–£10,000, while the CSL runs £7,000–£12,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M4 G82/G83 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S58B30T0 (base, 480 PS) — it's the lowest state of tune, placing the least stress on components. The Competition variants (510–530 PS) are a close second. The S58 is widely regarded as one of the strongest inline-six turbocharged units BMW has ever produced.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M4 G82/G83?

acceptedAnswer.text: S58 oil consumption between service intervals (particularly on track-used cars), fuel pump and injector issues, charge pipe and cooling system failures (especially on tuned cars), and — for the G83 Convertible — roof mechanism and weight-related issues.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M4 G82/G83 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the S58 is widely regarded as one of BMW's strongest and most reliable modern engines. It incorporates lessons from the S55 and B58, with a closed-deck design and forged internals. However, elevated oil consumption between services is a known characteristic, and early production cars may have fuel system teething issues.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW M4 G82/G83 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — all S58 variants are strong. However, the CSL's 550 PS calibration with higher boost pressure (2.1 bar vs 1.7 bar) puts more stress on components. It's not an 'avoid' engine — it's an 'understand what you're getting into' engine. If you want the most durable S58, choose the base 480 PS model.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my M4 G82/G83 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Elevated oil consumption (requiring top-ups between services), misfires, loss of power, fuel system warnings, or overheating are the key warning signs. Listen for unusual noises from the charge pipes. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW M4 G82/G83 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £60,000 M4, a £7,000 S58 recon is ~12% of the car's value — a repair decision, not a financial one. The 70% BMW threshold doesn't apply to cars of this value. M4s are never scrapped; they are restored.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW M4 G82/G83 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S58 can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing — it's one of BMW's most durable modern engines. Regular oil changes (8,000–10,000-mile intervals) are essential, particularly if the car is used on track. The CSL's higher boost pressure may reduce longevity, but data is limited.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the G82 and the G83?

acceptedAnswer.text: The G82 is the M4 Coupé (2020–present), while the G83 is the M4 Convertible (2021–present). Both share the same S58 engine and CLAR platform, but the G83 has a folding fabric roof and additional body strengthening, adding approximately 145 kg of weight. The G83's roof mechanism is a unique cost consideration — see each page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M4 G82/G83 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on the S58 unit. Tell us your variant (M4, M4 Competition, M4 CSL) and year and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW M specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW M4 G82/G83 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second-generation M4 — from the S58B30T0 in the base Coupé to the 530 PS Competition and the range-topping CSL. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the S58's oil consumption issues most guides miss entirely. Backed by 288+ real UK G82/G83 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW M4 G82/G83 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine variant fitted to the second-generation M4 — from the base 480 PS unit to the 530 PS Competition and the limited-edition CSL with its track-focused calibration.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All reliability ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet for the S58 engine family. The S58 is widely regarded as one of the strongest inline-six turbocharged units BMW has ever produced. It incorporates lessons from the S55 and B58 engines, suggesting a promising outlook for long-term performance. However, early owner reports indicate elevated oil consumption between service intervals, particularly on cars used frequently at high RPM or on track. The CSL's higher boost pressure (increased from 1.7 bar to 2.1 bar) adds additional stress to the engine.

overview.intro: The G82 is the second generation of the BMW M4 Coupé, unveiled in September 2020 and produced from November 2020. It succeeded the F82 M4 (2014–2020) with a more powerful S58 engine, a larger vertically-oriented grille, and a chassis that shares its CLAR platform with the G80 M3. The G83 Convertible followed in summer 2021, featuring a folding fabric roof and additional body strengthening that adds approximately 145 kg over the Coupé. A 2024 Life Cycle Impulse (LCI) facelift brought a 20 PS power increase for Competition models, taking output to 530 PS.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW CLAR (shared with G80 M3, G22 4 Series) • Predecessor: BMW M4 F82/F83 (2014–2020) • Successor: Currently in production (no successor announced) • Sibling body styles in this generation: G82 Coupé (2-door), G83 Convertible (2-door)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The G82/G83 M4 generated 288 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], concentrated on the S58 engine as these high-performance M cars age out of warranty and owners seek specialist maintenance and replacement advice.

items.quote: The S58 is widely regarded as one of the strongest inline-six turbocharged units BMW has ever produced. With a closed-deck design, forged crankshaft, and lessons learned from the S55 and B58, it delivers brutal power with surprising tractability. The 530 PS LCI Competition is the sweet spot — more power than the base car, but without the CSL's track-focused compromises.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the definitive modern M4 experience with the best balance of performance and reliability.

items.quote: The Competition is the M4 most buyers should choose — 510 PS (pre-LCI) or 530 PS (LCI) is more than enough for road and track. It's available with either rear-wheel drive or the rear-biased M xDrive system, giving you the choice of purist driving or all-weather usability. The CSL's extra 20–40 PS isn't worth the significant price premium for most buyers.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best all-round M4 without the CSL's track-focused compromises or price tag.

items.quote: The CSL's 550 PS calibration pushes the S58 harder than any other variant, with boost pressure increased from 1.7 bar to 2.1 bar. The higher output places additional stress on the turbochargers, fuel system, and cooling system. A full engine replacement on a CSL can exceed £12,000 — the most expensive S58 to repair.
items.whoItsFor: collectors who want the ultimate G82 M4 and have the budget to maintain it.

items.quote: The S58 is generally reliable, but early production cars (2020–2021) may have higher rates of oil consumption between service intervals. Some owners have reported fuel pump and injector issues. The crank hub design was a concern on the S55 but appears less problematic on the S58. However, aftermarket sources suggest excessive kickdowns and high torque loads can still increase the risk.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who understand that early production cars may require closer monitoring.

items.quote: The G83 Convertible is often overlooked in favour of the Coupé, but it offers a unique experience — open-air motoring with 530 PS and xDrive traction. The folding fabric roof adds 145 kg and some structural compromise, but the S58's torque means you won't notice the weight penalty in everyday driving. It's the M4 for buyers who want both performance and the wind-in-the-hair experience.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the M4 experience with the roof down.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Choice — lowest tune, most durable
Best Value — the one most buyers should choose
Solid Choice — more power, same engine
Enthusiast Only — ultimate performance, higher running costs

economicsRule.title: The G82/G83 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,000–£10,000 S58 recon against a £50,000–£90,000 M4 sits at 7–20% of the car's value — a repair decision, not a financial one. The 70% BMW threshold doesn't apply to cars of this value. However, the S58's oil consumption and potential fuel system issues mean that running costs can be significant — budget for regular oil top-ups and specialist servicing. The CSL's higher boost pressure (2.1 bar vs 1.7 bar) adds additional stress to the engine.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G82/G83 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: Base M4 (480 PS) — lowest state of tune, least stress on components.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G82/G83 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M4 CSL (550 PS) — higher boost pressure, track-focused calibration, expensive parts.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G82/G83 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M4 Competition (510–530 PS) — the sweet spot in the range, strongest residuals, the one enthusiasts want.

problems.title: S58 Oil Consumption
problems.rootCause: The S58 can exhibit elevated oil consumption between service intervals, particularly on cars used frequently at high RPM or on track. This is a characteristic of high-performance turbocharged engines and is not necessarily a sign of failure, but it requires monitoring.
problems.recommendation: Check oil levels regularly — BMW recommends checking at every fuel fill. Use the correct oil specification (BMW LL-04 or LL-12FE) and change oil every 8,000–10,000 miles if the car is used on track. A £100–£200 top-up routine is dramatically cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows oil starvation.

cta.label: Compare S58 oil service prices for your M4 G82/G83 →

problems.title: S58 Fuel Pump & Injector Issues
problems.rootCause: Problems can range from fuel pump failures to injector issues, which can lead to misfires and reduced performance. Solenoid-style direct injectors have proven to be reliable in the long run but are not exempt from failures. Carbon buildup on the tip can result in low-speed pre-ignition and atomisation problems.
problems.recommendation: If you experience misfires or reduced performance, have the fuel system inspected immediately. A £500–£1,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows if the engine runs lean and suffers damage. Use high-quality fuel and consider fuel system cleaner treatments.

cta.label: Compare S58 fuel system repair prices for your M4 G82/G83 →

problems.title: S58 Charge Pipe & Cooling System Issues
problems.rootCause: The G8X M2/M3/M4 models are equipped with an S58 engine featuring a Y-shaped plastic charge pipe. The charge air cooling system can suffer from rapid heat soak, particularly when the vehicle is used for track days. Owners of tuned S58 engines often confront this issue.
problems.recommendation: Consider upgrading the plastic charge pipe to an aluminium unit if you track your car or plan to tune it. A £250–£550 preventative upgrade is dramatically cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows if the plastic pipe fails and causes overheating.

cta.label: Compare S58 cooling system upgrades for your M4 G82/G83 →

problems.title: G83 Convertible Roof Mechanism & Weight
problems.rootCause: The G83's folding fabric roof adds approximately 145 kg of weight over the Coupé, requiring additional body strengthening. The roof mechanism itself is complex and can develop issues with the folding mechanism, seals, and hydraulic system.
problems.recommendation: Have the roof mechanism inspected regularly — a £200–£500 preventative service is dramatically cheaper than the £1,500+ repair that follows if the hydraulic system fails.

cta.label: Compare G83 roof mechanism repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: G82/G83 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are G82 (Coupé) and G83 (Convertible) second-generation M4 only, produced from November 2020 onwards. The G83 Convertible was launched in summer 2021. The M4 CSL (2022–2023) is a limited-production lightweight variant with 550 PS. The LCI facelift (2024–present) brought 530 PS to Competition models. xDrive (all-wheel drive) is available on Competition variants; base models are rear-wheel drive.
